source: Special Olympics Football Coaching Guide (2004) + Zimbabwe Grassroots Football Development Framework

phases.name: Foundation
phases.label: Foundation Phase
phases.focus: Fun, ball familiarity, basic movement and love for the game
phases.description: The Foundation Phase focuses on developing a love for football, basic motor skills, and ball familiarity. Winning is NOT important at this stage. Every child must have fun and get maximum ball contact.

training_guidelines.field_size: Small — 18 metres x 9 metres for games

physical_milestones: Can run with a ball at feet (slow jog)
Can kick a stationary ball at least 5 metres
Can stop a slow-rolling ball
Can change direction while dribbling (slow)
Developing basic coordination and balance
technical_milestones: Attempts to control ball (may bounce off foot)
Brings rolling ball under control using inside of foot
Dribbles forward using top of foot (toe down)
Can pass ball in general direction of a teammate
Kicks at goal — no consistency or power yet
Attempts to head stationary ball (very basic)
mental_milestones: Enjoys playing football
Can distinguish teammates from opponents most of the time
Knows which direction to kick
Beginning to understand basic rules (in/out, goals)
Responds to positive reinforcement

activities.focus: Juggling/Trapping — tap ball with feet alternately for 15 seconds
Shooting — kick ball at target (wall, fence, goal)
Control-Receiving — receive passes from family member
Dribbling — dribble around objects at home
Free play with a ball — just enjoy football

coaching_approach: Demonstrate rather than just telling — show the skill
Use gentle physical prompting when needed (hand on back for forward motion)
Maximum praise — especially for effort, not just success
Make everything a game — competition is between the player and themselves
Give every player maximum touches of the ball
do_not: Do not teach heading until U-10 minimum
Do not give abusive or destructive criticism

phases.name: Development
phases.label: Development Phase
phases.focus: Skill consolidation, tactical awareness, team play introduction
phases.description: The Development Phase builds on foundation skills. Players begin to understand team concepts, develop consistent technique and are introduced to tactical principles. Character development and respect for the game are emphasized.
physical_milestones: Can run with ball at pace
Developing aerobic base — can sustain 20–30 minute steady runs
Beginning anaerobic capacity — can sprint and recover
Correct plyometric movements — butt kicks, high knees, skipping
Adequate flexibility for football — groin, hamstring, calf, achilles
Basic strength — push-ups, sit-ups, lunges
technical_milestones: Brings rolling ball under control using inside of foot consistently
Good attempt at controlling air balls using chest
Dribbles with ball close — one metre or less
Uses inside and outside of foot to change direction
Passes with inside of foot for short passes, instep for longer
Shoots with instep for power — reasonable accuracy
Front block tackle executed correctly
Shows no fear heading ball — uses forehead
Goalkeeper attempts to get hands to ball and dive for ground shots
mental_milestones: Understands own role on the team
Beginning to support player with the ball
Can follow simple positional instructions
Understands basic restarts (kick-off, goal kick, corner, throw-in, free kick)
Shows respect for teammates, opponents and referee
Developing concentration for full match duration
Beginning to use mental imagery for skill practice
tactical_milestones: Knows their position (wing, striker, defender, goalkeeper)
Attempts to spread out rather than crowd the ball
Beginning to support the player with the ball
Understands 'play safe from the back' principle
Beginning to shoot when opportunity arises

activities.focus: Juggling/Trapping — set personal best record
Shooting — 20 shots at target, aim for corners
Control-Receiving — 2-touch drills with partner
Dribbling — cone course with both feet
Fitness — 20-minute steady jog with ball
Street football / mini game

coaching_approach: Use the 'freeze' technique in games to teach principles
Begin introducing game conditions (no dribble in own half, etc.)
Explain WHY each technique or principle is important
Use small-sided games as the primary learning tool
Ask players questions — What position are you? Where should you be?
Introduce mental imagery — visualize the skill step by step

phases.name: Performance
phases.label: Performance Phase
phases.focus: Technical refinement, tactical sophistication, physical development, competitive readiness
phases.description: The Performance Phase prepares players for serious competitive football. Technical excellence, physical conditioning, tactical understanding and mental toughness are all developed systematically.
physical_milestones: Good cardiovascular capacity — sustains pace for 90 minutes
Can sprint, recover and sprint again multiple times
Full flexibility routine — all major muscle groups
Adequate strength — push-ups, lunges, medicine ball throws
Plyometric competency — rhythm drills, power exercises
Body awareness and proprioception well developed
technical_milestones: Controls ball with all surfaces under pressure
Dribbles with head up — aware of game around them
Passes accurately over short and long distances using both feet
Shoots with power and accuracy — can volley
Executes all four types of tackle correctly and safely
Headers — standing, jumping, clearing and attacking
Goalkeeper — reads game, commands box, distributes accurately
mental_milestones: Full concentration for 90-minute match
Composed under pressure — does not rush
Confident in own ability
Uses mental imagery regularly
Respectful winner and gracious loser
Motivates teammates positively
Can self-correct technique during a game
tactical_milestones: Executes all five game principles consistently
Understands 11-a-side formation and own role within it
Makes correct decisions: pass, dribble or shoot — and when
Can execute all basic restarts correctly without prompting
Provides consistent support to player with ball

activities.focus: Strength circuit — push-ups, lunges, sit-ups, medicine ball
Ball work — juggling, dribbling, shooting 30 minutes
Anaerobic fitness — fartlek run or shuttle runs
Technical — passing and control with partner
Mental training — imagery session 10 minutes + rest
Game or free training

phases.name: Professional
phases.label: Professional Phase
phases.focus: Peak performance, competition preparation, professional standards, leadership
phases.description: The Professional Phase represents the peak of a player's development. Training is systematic, periodized and competition-focused. Players are responsible for own fitness maintenance and contribute to team culture.

session_structure.component: Full game or extended practice match

training_guidelines.periodization: Volume and intensity vary over season. Reduce plyometrics to one light session per week during peak competition. Stop strenuous plyometric training 7–10 days before playoffs.

physical_milestones: Peak cardiovascular fitness
Sport-specific strength and power
Complete flexibility program as daily habit
Optimized body composition for position
Full plyometric program executed correctly
Fast recovery between training sessions
technical_milestones: Technical excellence across all skills
Executes skills under maximum pressure
Ambidextrous — equally competent with both feet
Advanced techniques — volleys, headers from crosses, sliding tackles
Complete goalkeeper — command of box, set pieces, distribution
mental_milestones: Mental imagery as standard preparation routine
Complete composure under match pressure
Strong leadership and positive team communication
Resilient — recovers immediately from errors
Respectful winner and gracious loser at all times
Role model for younger players
tactical_milestones: Reads the game several moves ahead
Knows when to break tactical patterns and improvise
Effective player in both pressing and defensive block systems

weekly_home_training.note: Players at this level maintain personal training schedules. Recovery and mental preparation are equally important as physical training.

activities.focus: Strength — full gym or bodyweight circuit
Ball mastery — individual technical work 45 minutes
Anaerobic conditioning — sprint intervals, fartlek
Tactical study + mental imagery
Recovery — light jog, stretching, cool down
Competition or rest depending on schedule

age_appropriate_notes.heading: Do not teach or stress headers until U-11. Use soft or underinflated ball when first introducing. Medical studies have found extensive heading can cause brain damage.
age_appropriate_notes.slide_tackling: Only teach to higher ability players (Performance phase and above). Must be done correctly — all contact on the ball from the side.
age_appropriate_notes.full_sided_game: 5-a-side appropriate from Foundation phase. 11-a-side only appropriate from Performance phase (U15+) with good basic skills, speed and endurance.
age_appropriate_notes.plyometrics: Begin with simple rhythm drills. Introduce power exercises only once rhythm drills are mastered. Volume and intensity always low to moderate for youth players.
age_appropriate_notes.strength_training: Bodyweight exercises only until Performance phase. No weight room until late Performance / Professional phase.
